coredata.dc:description: This study investigates synthesizing and characterizing dual-doped carbon dots (NCD) using malic acid, oxalic acid, diammonium tartrate, and rare earth metal salts as precursors. The research highlights the impact of varying dopant ratios on the NCD structural, morphological, optical properties, and antimicrobial activity. Results demonstrate that adjusting dopant ratios significantly influences photoluminescence behavior. Notably, malic acid-oxalic acid co-doped CD exhibit a red-shift in emission from 443 nm to 477 nm as oxalic acid content increases from 3 g to 5 g, attributed to additional surface defects and carboxyl groups. Conversely, the diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid system shows a slight blue-shift from 478 nm to 475 nm due to sp³ defects that widen the bandgap. Emission intensity is notably higher for the diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid system, ranging from 30,620 to 66,260 counts, compared to the malic acid-oxalic acid system (6,260 to 42,708 counts), underscoring nitrogen doping's role in enhancing luminescence, incorporating rare earth metals like terbium and europium results in crystalline TbN and EuN phases, improving photoluminescent properties and new radiative pathways. The NCD demonstrate significant antimicrobial activity under UV radiation, with terbium and europium co-doped NCD showing enhanced microbial inactivation compared to undoped NCD. The antimicrobial efficacy was attributed to the enhanced generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) under UV light, effectively inactivating bacterial strains. These findings indicate that the dual-doped NCD possess tunable optical properties and enhanced antimicrobial activity, making them suitable candidates for applications in bioimaging, sensing, and disinfection technologies.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study investigates synthesizing and characterizing dual-doped carbon dots (NCD) using malic acid, oxalic acid, diammonium tartrate, and rare earth metal salts as precursors. The research highlights the impact of varying dopant ratios on the NCD structural, morphological, optical properties, and antimicrobial activity. Results demonstrate that adjusting dopant ratios significantly influences photoluminescence behavior. Notably, malic acid-oxalic acid co-doped CD exhibit a red-shift in emission from 443 nm to 477 nm as oxalic acid content increases from 3 g to 5 g, attributed to additional surface defects and carboxyl groups. Conversely, the diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid system shows a slight blue-shift from 478 nm to 475 nm due to sp³ defects that widen the bandgap. Emission intensity is notably higher for the diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid system, ranging from 30,620 to 66,260 counts, compared to the malic acid-oxalic acid system (6,260 to 42,708 counts), underscoring nitrogen doping's role in enhancing luminescence, incorporating rare earth metals like terbium and europium results in crystalline TbN and EuN phases, improving photoluminescent properties and new radiative pathways. The NCD demonstrate significant antimicrobial activity under UV radiation, with terbium and europium co-doped NCD showing enhanced microbial inactivation compared to undoped NCD. The antimicrobial efficacy was attributed to the enhanced generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) under UV light, effectively inactivating bacterial strains. These findings indicate that the dual-doped NCD possess tunable optical properties and enhanced antimicrobial activity, making them suitable candidates for applications in bioimaging, sensing, and disinfection technologies.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials have garnered significant research attention in recent years due to their unique size-dependent properties [1–4], chemical versatility [5], and potential applications across diverse scientific fields [5,6]. In particular, a class of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials called carbon dots (NCD) has risen to prominence owing to their high optical performance [7], biocompatibility [8], chemical inertness [9], and facile synthesis [10]. NCD are quasi-spherical nanoparticles composed of amorphous to nanocrystalline carbon domains with abundant surface passivating groups [11,12]. With lateral dimensions below 10 nm [13], NCD exhibit properties associated with quantum confinement [14], most notably strong photoluminescence in the visible spectrum [15]. This has opened up many NCD applications in optoelectronics [16], bioimaging [17], photocatalysis [18], sensing [19], and nanomedicine [20]. The optical properties of NCD are governed by the electron transitions occurring within the carbon core and at the surface defects/functional groups [21,22]. By tuning NCD' composition and surface chemistry, their fluorescence emission can be tailored across the visible spectrum [23].

Furthermore, doping NCD with inorganic elements provides an additional means of modulating their optical [24], electronic [25], and chemical properties [24,26]. In particular, nitrogen doping has dramatically enhanced the photoluminescence quantum yield while enabling visible color tunability [27]. Recently, attention has turned to dual-doped NCD incorporating nitrogen and a second dopant element, such as sulfur [28], phosphorus [29], or metal [30]. The complex interplay between the two dopants leads to emergent properties distinct from singly doped NCD [31]. However, dual-doping's synergistic effects on NCD' optical characteristics remain poorly understood [26,32,33]. Among various synthetic approaches, the hydrothermal carbonization technique has proven to be a simple yet versatile method for preparing NCD and dual-doped NCD [28,34]. Hydrothermal synthesis involves heating aqueous solutions of organic precursors and dopant sources in a sealed autoclave at temperatures above the boiling point of water [35–37]. Under hydrothermal conditions, simultaneous carbonization and passivation reactions produce surface-functionalized NCD with excellent photoluminescence properties [22,38,39]. The technique offers fine control over the reaction conditions like temperature, pressure, and reaction time to tailor the optical features [40]. Furthermore, the precursor composition and dopant sources can be systematically varied to achieve dual-doped NCD with desired properties [41–43]. This work synthesized NCD and nitrogen-doped NCD (NCD) via the hydrothermal carbonization of organic acid precursors, namely malic acid, oxalic acid, and diammonium tartrate. Terbium (Tb) and europium (Eu) salts were introduced as rare-earth metal dopants during synthesis for dual-doping.

Terbium and europium were chosen as representative lanthanide elements due to their unique photophysical properties for luminescent applications. The objective was to establish structure-property relationships between precursor composition and the optical characteristics of the resulting dual-doped NCD. The N-doping aimed to modulate the visible color emission, while the rare-earth metals were expected to introduce new intra-bandgap states and synergistically combine with N-doping to enhance luminescence efficiency. A key focus was exploring how the N-dopant content interplays with the concentration of Tb and Eu to influence the optical and structural properties. By systematically varying the precursor ratios, dual-doped NCD with bright, tunable fluorescence emissions ranging from violet to red were targeted. Recent research has highlighted significant findings in environmental pollution and advanced material applications [44,45]. Complemented by exploring innovative materials for environmental application [46–49].

The hydrothermal synthesis technique enabled careful control over the reaction conditions to optimize optical performance. Photoluminescence spectroscopy was utilized as the primary tool for assessing the visible light emission properties of the synthesized dual-doped NCD. Structural characterization techniques like X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were employed to correlate the optical features with the chemical composition, bonding configuration, and microstructure of the dual-doped NCD. This work provides novel insights into the synergistic effects of nitrogen and rare-earth metal co-doping on the properties of NCD produced via hydrothermal synthesis. The insights gained from this study propel the domain of surface and interface science by providing a deeper understanding of how dual doping influences surface chemistry and defect dynamics in carbon-based materials. This study presents several novel findings that significantly advance the knowledge of dual-doped carbon dots, distinguishing it from existing literature. Notably, it demonstrates the synergistic effects of nitrogen and rare earth metal dopants (terbium and europium) on enhancing photoluminescence, surpassing previous works like Sudolská et al. [50], which focused primarily on single doping. Additionally, our research reveals marked antimicrobial activity under UV illumination, showcasing significantly improved microbial inactivation compared to undoped carbon dots, which were not investigated before. Furthermore, the study provides deeper mechanistic insights into how varying dopant ratios influence emission characteristics through surface state modulation—an aspect less explored in literature, such as by Esmaeili et al. [51]. By tailoring the optical properties for broader applications in bioimaging, sensing, and disinfection technologies, this research expands the functional scope of carbon dots beyond the limited capabilities emphasized by studies like Zheng et al. [52]. The primary objective of this study is to synthesize and characterize dual-doped carbon dots (NCD) incorporating nitrogen and rare-earth metals (terbium and europium) to achieve enhanced photoluminescent and antimicrobial properties. By systematically varying the dopant ratios and precursor compositions, the study aims to investigate the interplay between doping elements and the resulting structural, morphological, and optical characteristics of the NCD. Key aspects of this work include tailoring emission wavelengths through dopant modulation, exploring the effects of nitrogen doping on fluorescence efficiency, and elucidating the role of rare-earth metals in enhancing luminescence and antimicrobial activity.

The materials used in this study were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich, ensuring high purity levels for reliable results. Malic acid (≥99.0 % purity) and oxalic acid (≥99.5 % purity) served as key carbon precursors, contributing to the structural integrity and functionalization of the carbon dots. Diammonium tartrate (≥99.0 % purity) was utilized for its nitrogen content, enhancing the optical properties through doping. Terbium (III) nitrate hexahydrate and europium (III) nitrate pentahydrate, both with ≥99.9 % purity supplied from Sigma-Aldrich, were selected as rare earth metal dopants to improve luminescence. Deionized water was the solvent to dissolve the precursors, ensuring a homogeneous reaction environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots (CD) by hydrothermal/solvothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 outlines the detailed process for preparing Carbon Dots (CD) using a hydrothermal/solvothermal method. This method selects malic acid and oxalic acid as precursor compounds. The synthesis begins by dissolving precisely weighed amounts of malic acid and oxalic acid in 20 ml of water, forming a homogeneous solution. The hydrothermal/solvothermal method involves subjecting the solution to controlled high-temperature conditions, specifically at 180 °C, for two hours. This process induces a combustion reaction, leading to the formation of Carbon Dots. Using water as a solvent ensures a conducive environment for the response and aids in the uniform dispersion of the precursor compounds. The varying proportions of malic acid and oxalic acid in each sample (samples 1 to 5) provide a systematic exploration of how different precursor ratios influence the characteristics of the resulting CD. The hydrothermal/solvothermal synthesis route ensures the controlled and efficient formation of CD with potential applications in various fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) by hydrothermal/solvothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots (NCD), the hydrothermal/solvothermal method is again employed, with a distinct precursor combination of Diammonium Tartrate and oxalic acid. Samples 6 to 10 showcase the preparation of NCD through this method. The process is initiated by dissolving precise amounts of Diammonium Tartrate and oxalic acid in 20 ml of water, creating a homogeneous solution. Like the CD synthesis, the solution is subjected to a controlled heating process at 180 °C for 2 hours. The combustion of the dissolved precursor compounds results in Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots, with nitrogen incorporation from Diammonium Tartrate. Water as a solvent facilitates the reaction and ensures uniform dispersion of the precursors. The deliberate inclusion of nitrogen in the NCD introduces unique properties, potentially enhancing their applications in various fields, including sensing and imaging. The systematic variation in the proportions of Diammonium Tartrate and oxalic acid allows for a thorough investigation into the impact of nitrogen doping on the structure and functionalities of the synthesized carbon dots. This hydrothermal/solvothermal method offers a versatile approach to tailor NCD' properties for specific applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of terbium (Tb) and europium (Eu) doped carbon dots via hydrothermal method
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of terbium (Tb) and europium (Eu) doped carbon dots (CD) via a hydrothermal method involves using nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD) along with terbium and europium salts as rare-earth metal dopants. This method allows precise control over reaction conditions, enabling the tailoring of optical properties. Terbium and europium were selected for their unique photophysical properties, which can enhance luminescence efficiency when combined with nitrogen. Terbium-doped Nitrogen Carbon Dots (TbNCD) synthesis begins with dissolving 20 mg of Terbium(III) nitrate in 20 ml of water to form a homogeneous solution. Next, 0.4 gm of NCD is added to the Tb solution. After two hours, this mixture is subjected to hydrothermal conditions in a sealed autoclave at 180 °C. Under these conditions, simultaneous carbonization and passivation reactions occur, producing surface-functionalized TbNCD. The synthesis process of Europium-doped Nitrogen Carbon Dots (EuNCD) is similar to that of the TbNCD. Instead of terbium salts, europium salts are introduced into the solution. The europium precursors are dissolved in 20 ml of water, and the resulting solution undergoes hydrothermal treatment in a sealed autoclave at 180 °C for two hours. This process results in the formation of surface-functionalized EuNCD. The hydrothermal methods in a controlled environment ensure the desired doping and functionalization. This results in TbNCD and EuNCD with enhanced luminescent properties due to the rare-earth metal dopants and nitrogen presence.

Using three isolates of pathogenic bacteria and unicellular fungi (yeast) obtained from the culture collections in the Drug Microbiology Lab., NCRRT, Cairo, Egypt, the antimicrobial activity of the as-synthesized NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD was investigated. The medical operation rooms' surfaces and exteriors, such as beds, walls, and doors, were cleaned of pathogenic microorganisms. The studied bacterial isolates were identified and discriminated in our previous work [53], and their capacity to produce biofilms was also examined. Staphylococcus aureus (Gram-positive bacteria), Escherichia coli (Gram-negative bacteria), and Candida albicans (unicellular fungi) were the tested microbial strains. The bacterial inoculums have been fixed using a fixed 600 nm UV–Vis. spectrophotometer, yielding 0.5 McFarland (1–3) ×108 CFU/mL [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microbial growth curve assay: Kinetic study
ce:section.ce:para: The growth curve experiment was used by Huang et al. [55], to assess the effects of NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD against the growth of S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans. In 5.0 ml nutrient broth tubes, the microbial culture has been modified to 0.5 McFarland (1 × 108 CFU/mL). Separate equal quantities of NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD were added to each tube under examination. Up to 24 h after treatment, absorbance of the growth of bacteria and yeast was measured at intervals of every two hours (wavelength of 600 nm). The normal growth curve was obtained by comparing the mean of duplicate readings across hourly intervals.

Bacterial and yeast cultures were incubated for two hours after reaching a standard concentration of 0.5 McFarland (1 × 108 CFU/mL). NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD were added to the designated tubes. The antibacterial efficiency of the synthesized NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD against S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans was evaluated using the optical density method after UV illumination, compared with a non-UV [56]. Two sets of tubes were prepared, with one set containing nanocomposites that were not exposed to UV light, while the other set included those that were. The synthesized NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD were exposed to different durations of 0, 15, 30, 45, 60, and 75 min. The samples were tested at 37 °C, and the strength of the disturbances was set at 6.9 mW cm−2. The turbidity of the samples was measured at 600 nm. These procedures align with the previous work [57], and Eq. (1) defined the inhibition percentage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of synthesized carbon dots on protein leakage from bacterial cell membranes
ce:section.ce:para: A 0.5 McFarland (1 × 108 CFU/mL) pure 18-hour bacterial and yeast culture was set, and 100 µL was injected into 10 mL of the nutritional broth that included scattered and well-sonicated NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD at different concentrations (0.125, 0.25, 0.5, and 1.0 mg/mL). The control was a broth infused with culture but did not include the synthesized Carbon Dots. After five hours of incubation at 37 °C, all treated samples were centrifuged for fifteen minutes at 5500 rpm [58]. One mL of Bradford reagent was mixed with 100 µL of supernatant for each sample. The optical density was determined after 10 min of dark incubation at 595 nm [58].

To determine the statistical analysis of the obtained results, Duncan's multiple sequence studies were utilized to arrange the results of a one-way ANOVA (at P < 0.05) [59]. The data was examined and analyzed using SPSS version 15.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical Properties of synthesized carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra provide significant insights into how the dual dopants influence the optical properties of the synthesized carbon dots. Analyzing the effects of varying the dopant concentrations reveals key structure-property relationships. Fig. 1 illustrates the emission characteristics of two dual-doped carbon dot systems: malic acid-oxalic acid and diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid. In the malic acid-oxalic acid dual-doped samples (1–5), increasing the oxalic acid content from 3 g to 5 g while keeping malic acid amounts fixed resulted in red-shifted emissions ranging from 443 nm to 477 nm. This bathochromic shift of 34 nm can be ascribed to generating more surface defects and oxygen functionalities from higher oxalic acid levels during carbonization. The additional carboxyl groups likely create more emissive traps on the carbon dot surface, stabilizing excited state charge carriers and lowering the band gap to give red-shifted emissions. Interestingly, a similar trend was observed when holding the oxalic acid quantity fixed while incrementing the malic acid dosage from 1 g to 5 g, which induced a bathochromic shift from 443 nm to 477 nm. This indicates that malic acid doping sensitizes the optical transitions by contributing hydroxyl/carbonyl sites as surface state modulators. The highest intensity obtained in this system was 42,708 counts for 1 g malic acid-5 g oxalic acid. The diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid co-doped samples (6–10) exhibited slightly different behavior. Increasing oxalic acid amounts from 3 g to 5 g at constant diammonium tartrate prompted a slight hypsochromic shift from 478 nm to 475 nm. While this 3 nm blue emission shift is relatively minor, it suggests that higher oxalic acid concentrations might introduce more sp3 defects, which could restrict π-conjugation and slightly widen the band gap. However, raising the diammonium tartrate content from 1 g to 5 g at fixed 5 g oxalic acid elicited a substantial bathochromic shift from 449 nm to 475 nm. This 26 nm red-shift can be credited to extra hydroxyl groups from diammonium tartrate, aiding better surface passivation by hydrogen bonding and producing more emissive traps.

Comparing the two dopant systems reveals that diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid doping imparted universally higher emission intensities, ranging from 30,620 to 66,260 counts, versus 6260 to 42,708 counts for malic acid-oxalic acid. This underscores the vital role of the nitrogen-containing diammonium tartrate in boosting luminescence by increasing electron-donating amino groups. The maximum intensity achieved was 66,260 counts for 1 g diammonium tartrate with 5 g oxalic acid. The wavelength trends differ noticeably between the two dual dopant combinations. While higher oxalic acid amounts tend to red-shift emissions in the malic acid-oxalic system, they induce slight hypsochromic shifts in the diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid case. This divergence highlights the complex interplay between the two dopants in accessing distinct emissive sites and governing surface chemistry. The optimal oxalic acid level appears to be 5 g for both dual dopant systems. Still, diammonium tartrate-doped carbon dots offered universally brighter emissions owing to synergies between hydroxyl/amino functional groups and carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The XRD analysis of dual doping carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 a shows the XRD analysis of Nitrogen atoms doped Carbon dots. The XRD pattern of the nitrogen-doped carbon material exhibits several notable features that provide insights into its structural properties and degree of graphitization. The broad peak centered around 2θ = 24.08° corresponds to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon, a characteristic signature of turbostratic or disordered graphitic structures. The broadness of the (002) peak, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 0.2628°, indicates a significant degree of disorder and defects within the graphitic layers. This is commonly observed in nitrogen-doped carbon materials due to incorporating nitrogen atoms, which disrupt the planar sp2 hybridization of the carbon atoms and introduce distortions in the graphitic structure. The position of the (002) peak at 2θ = 24.08° corresponds to an interplanar spacing (d002) of 3.69277 Å, which is slightly larger than the ideal value for pristine graphite (3.354 Å). This increase in the d002 spacing can be attributed to nitrogen atoms within the graphitic layers and the introduction of structural defects and distortions. The intensity of the (002) peak (I/I1 = 100, Intensity = 173 counts) suggests a significant contribution from the graphitic component in the material. However, the absence of well-defined higher-order diffraction peaks, such as (004) and (006), indicates a low degree of graphitization or a predominantly turbostratic structure. In addition to the (002) peak, several other peaks are observed in the XRD pattern, such as those at 2θ = 16.0797° (d = 5.50758 Å, I/I1 = 87) and 2θ = 26.8169° (d = 3.32181 Å, I/I1 = 76). These peaks could be attributed to residual catalysts, support materials, or other phases formed during synthesis.

Fig. 2b shows the XRD analysis of Terbium (Tb) and Nitrogen atoms doped carbon dots. The prominent peaks observed are at 2θ values of 40.6328°, 23.9718°, and 20.1456° corresponding to d-spacings of 2.21858 Å, 3.70923 Å, and 4.40425 Å, respectively. These are the diffraction peaks indicating the presence of highly crystalline phases. The high intensity of this peak suggests a preferential orientation in the (111) direction. The peaks at 2θ = 23.9718° (d = 3.70923 Å) and 2θ = 20.1456° (d = 4.40425 Å) correspond to the (111) and (200) planes of face-centered cubic TbN respectively [60], further confirming the presence of TbN crystalline phase. Several other peaks match the TbN reference pattern, such as those at 2θ = 27.718° (d = 3.21561 Å) for the (220) plane, and 2θ = 33.118° (d = 2.70223 Å) for the (311) plane, indicating the formation of polycrystalline TbN. The broad peak around 2θ = 25° can be attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon (JCPDS #41–1487) [61], suggesting the presence of carbon dots with some graphitic character. The absence of sharp peaks corresponding to metallic Tb suggests that terbium is present in TbN rather than as a separate metallic phase. The peak at 2θ ≈ 20.1456° (d ≈ 4.40425 Å) has the highest intensity, which could be attributed to the (002) or (100) plane of a hexagonal or cubic carbon-based structure, respectively. The peaks at 2θ ≈ 18.6820° (d ≈ 4.74586 Å) and 2θ ≈ 64.7400° (d ≈ 1.43878 Å) may correspond to the (111) and (220) planes, respectively, of a face-centered cubic (FCC) terbium oxide (Tb2O3) phase. Fig. 2c shows the XRD analysis of Europium (Eu) and Nitrogen atoms doped carbon dots. The diffraction peaks are observed at 2θ values of 23.9963° (d = 3.70550 Å), 20.1550° (d = 4.40221 Å), and 27.3244° (d = 3.26126 Å), indicating the presence of highly crystalline phases. The peak at 2θ = 23.9963° (d = 3.70550 Å) corresponds to the (111) plane of face-centered cubic (FCC) EuN (europium nitride) [62], which is the most intense peak, suggesting a preferential orientation along the (111) direction. The peaks at 2θ = 20.1550° (d = 4.40221 Å) and 2θ = 27.3244° (d = 3.26126 Å) match the (200) and (220) planes of FCC EuN, respectively [63], further confirming the presence of the EuN crystalline phase. Several other peaks observed, such as those at 2θ = 32.0200° (d = 2.79291 Å) and 2θ = 40.6020° (d = 2.22019 Å), correspond to the (311) and (222) planes of EuN, respectively, indicating the formation of polycrystalline EuN nanoparticles. The broad peak around 2θ = 25° can be attributed to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, suggesting the presence of carbon dots with some graphitic character. No distinct peaks are observed for metallic or other europium-based phases, implying that europium is present solely in EuN and does not exist as a separate metallic phase. The absence of sharp peaks corresponding to nitrogen-based phases suggests that nitrogen is incorporated into the EuN crystal lattice rather than as a separate phase. The XRD analyses of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Fig. 2a) reveal a predominantly disordered graphitic structure, evidenced by a broad (002) peak and the absence of higher-order diffraction peaks. In contrast, terbium and europium-doped carbon dots (Figs. 2b and 2c) exhibit well-defined diffraction peaks, indicating higher crystallinity and the presence of respective nitride phases, with additional features suggesting the coexistence of carbon dots with some graphitic character. Fig. 2a lacks well-defined higher-order diffraction peaks, suggesting low graphitization, whereas Figs. 2b and 2c display multiple distinct peaks matching the reference patterns of face-centered cubic TbN and EuN, respectively, indicative of higher crystallinity and the formation of polycrystalline nitride nanoparticles dispersed in a carbon matrix. The XRD patterns indicate the successful doping of nitrogen, terbium, and europium atoms into carbon dot structures, with the formation of crystalline nitride phases like TbN and EuN along with turbostratic graphitic carbon in the terbium and europium doped samples. However, only a disordered graphitic structure was observed for the nitrogen-doped carbon dots without distinct nitride peaks. This suggests a lower degree of crystallinity than the rare earth-metal-doped carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FT-IR analysis of dual doping carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectrum of Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots (NCD) in Fig. 3 a reveals distinctive peaks providing insights into the chemical composition and bonding structures. The broad peak observed at 3677 cm⁻¹ is attributed to OH stretching vibrations, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups on the carbon dot surfaces, possibly from alcohol or phenol moieties. The bands around 3000 cm⁻¹ (2984 cm⁻¹) correspond to sp2 hybridized CH stretching vibrations, suggesting the existence of graphitic carbon domains within the structure. Peaks in the 1700–1600 cm⁻¹ (1657 cm⁻¹) denote C = O stretching vibrations associated with carbonyl or carboxyl groups, highlighting the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups. Below 1500 cm⁻¹, the bands signify the presence of single CC bonds, indicative of diverse oxygen and nitrogen functionalities, showcasing the structural complexity of NCD. The Terbium-Doped Nitrogen Carbon Dots (TbNCD) in Fig. 3b exhibits distinct FT-IR peaks that shed light on their unique chemical composition. The broad peak at 3639 cm⁻¹ indicates OH stretching vibrations akin to the hydroxyl groups in NCD and TbNCD. The bands around 3000 cm⁻¹ (3057 cm⁻¹) correspond to sp2 hybridized CH stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of graphitic carbon domains[46].. Notably, the 2372 cm⁻¹ and 2259 cm⁻¹ peaks are associated with a nitrile group (CN) and carbon-carbon triple bond stretching vibrations (C), providing evidence of nitrogen-containing functionalities. Peaks around 1700–1600 cm⁻¹ (1638 cm⁻¹) denote C = C stretching vibrations, while peaks at 1532 cm⁻¹ and 1349 cm⁻¹ are indicative of NH bending vibrations and CN stretching vibrations, respectively, highlighting the presence of amino groups. The sharp peaks at 660 and 494 cm⁻¹ are specific to TbNCD, assigned to stretching and deformation modes associated with Tb-O and Tb-N bonds, providing clear evidence of terbium incorporation. Europium-doped nitrogen Carbon Dots (EuNCD) in Fig. 3c show distinctive FT-IR peaks, revealing the intricate molecular details of these nanomaterials. The broad peak at 3501 cm⁻¹ signifies OH stretching vibrations, similar to hydroxyl groups in NCD and TbNCD. Peaks around 3000 cm⁻¹ (3161 cm⁻¹ and 2829 cm⁻¹) correspond to sp2 hybridized CH stretching vibrations, suggesting the presence of graphitic carbon domains. Peaks at 2191 cm⁻¹ and 2039 cm⁻¹ indicate CN stretching vibrations and C = O stretching vibrations, respectively, emphasizing the role of nitrogen and carbonyl groups in the structure of EuNCD. Peaks around 1700–1600 cm⁻¹ (1647 cm⁻¹) denote C = C stretching vibrations, while peaks at 1547 cm⁻¹ and 1432 cm⁻¹ indicate NH bending vibrations and CN stretching vibrations, respectively, pointing to the presence of amino groups[64]. The 1179 cm⁻¹ and 912 cm⁻¹ peaks correspond to CO stretching and CH bending vibrations in aromatic rings. The specific peaks at 779 cm⁻¹ and 612 cm⁻¹ indicate Eu-O and Eu-N modes, confirming the successful doping of the europium nitride phase in the carbon dot structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The SEM analysis of dual doping carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM image in Fig. 4 a shows the pure N-doped carbon dots (NCD) morphology. The NCD have an irregular lamellar structure with folding and wrinkling evident on the surface. This results in a porous, non-uniform morphology with significant roughness. The conglomerated nature and resemblance between NCD particles indicate possible particle aggregation. The lack of distinct individual particles suggests the NCD may tend to stack or aggregate together. After incorporating Tb and Eu atoms, the morphology of the NCD changes significantly in Figs. 4b and 4c. The particles appear more packed and agglomerated compared to pure NCD. For both TbNCD and EuNCD, the particles seem to be more densely clustered with reduced porosity between particles. This suggests the dopants may induce increased particle aggregation. However, some porous regions are still observed in the TbNCD and EuNCD composites, indicating that the dopants help mitigate aggregation. The incorporation of Tb and Eu appears to have reduced the lamellar stacking of the NCD, leading to a more granular but still agglomerated structure. The SEM data suggests dopant incorporation impacts the surface morphology of the NCD, making them more prone to aggregation and disrupting the lamellar stacking observed in pure NCD. The porous regions imply the composites are still partially agglomerated. The EDX analysis in Fig. 5 a confirms the presence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in the NCD sample. Carbon constitutes 39 at%, indicating it is the most abundant element, while nitrogen and oxygen are detected at 8 at% and 52 at%, respectively.

The significant nitrogen content proves the doping of nitrogen atoms into the carbon dot structure. The high 52 at% oxygen suggests surface oxidation of the carbon dots. In Fig. 5b, the TbNCD, carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and terbium are observed in the TbNCD through the TbL peak at 9 at%. This verifies that terbium is successfully incorporated into the doped carbon dots. Nitrogen is present at 10 at%, suggesting it likely forms TbN bonds with some of the terbium. The significant carbon content (46 at%) indicates the TbN nanoparticles are anchored and stabilized on graphitic carbon dot surfaces, while surface oxidation is still evident from 35 at% oxygen. In Fig. 5c the additional europium peak (EuL) at 9 at% confirms the doping of europium atoms into the carbon dot matrix. With 44 % carbon and 8 % nitrogen, the EDX data matches well with a composition of EuN nanoparticles grown on carbon supports. The surface oxide percentage is slightly lower in EuNCD versus TbNCD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The TEM analysis of dual doping carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 6a presents a TEM image of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCD). The image reveals a collection of small, spherical particles with a relatively uniform size distribution ranging from 3 to 5 nm. These particles appear well-dispersed without significant agglomeration, indicating good colloidal stability. Fig. 6b offers another view of the NCD, focusing on a smaller cluster of particles. This image reinforces the observations from Fig. 6a, showing the NCD consistent spherical morphology and narrow size distribution. Fig. 6c provides a wider field of view of the NCD, allowing for a better assessment of their overall distribution and uniformity. This image, in conjunction with the XRD data showing an increased d002 spacing of 3.69277 Å (compared to 3.354 Å for pristine graphite), provides strong evidence for the successful incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the carbon dot structure, resulting in a disordered, nitrogen-doped carbon material. Fig. 6d show the TEM image of terbium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (TbNCD). In contrast to the NCD, these particles exhibit a quasi-spherical morphology with a slightly larger size distribution of 3–6 nm. The most striking feature of this image is the presence of visible lattice fringes, indicating a higher degree of crystallinity than the NCD.

The observed lattice spacing of approximately 0.361 nm is attributed to the (111) interplanar d-spacing of cubic TbN. This observation partially aligns with the XRD data, which indicated the presence of a TbN crystalline phase. However, the specific d-spacing observed in the TEM doesn't directly correspond to the prominent peaks in the XRD pattern, suggesting a more complex crystal structure than initially interpreted. This image provides crucial evidence for the formation of TbN nanocrystallites within the carbon dot structure, confirming the successful co-doping of terbium and nitrogen. Fig. 6f highlighting the cubic morphology and the presence of well-defined lattice fringes. The consistent appearance of lattice fringes across multiple particles in this image suggests a high degree of crystallinity throughout the sample, which aligns with the sharp, intense peaks observed in the XRD pattern. The larger particle size and distinct cubic shape, compared to the NCD and TbNCD, indicate that the incorporation of europium significantly influences the growth and crystallization process of the carbon dots. Fig. 6e presents a TEM image of europium and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (EuNCD). These particles display a distinct cubic morphology, the size distribution is notably more extensive, ranging from 3 to 9 nm, indicating a different growth mechanism or increased crystal formation due to europium doping. The image shows clear lattice fringes, signifying a high degree of crystallinity. The measured d-spacing of approximately 0.269 nm can be indexed to the (111) planes of cubic EuN. This strongly corroborates the XRD data, which identifies the presence of a face-centered cubic (FCC) EuN crystalline phase. The cubic shape and well-defined lattice fringes are consistent with the sharp, intense peaks observed in the XRD pattern for EuNCD. This image provides direct visual evidence of the formation of EuN nano cubic within the carbon dot structure, demonstrating the successful incorporation of europium into the nitrogen-doped carbon matrix. Conjunction with the XRD data showing multiple distinct peaks matching the reference patterns of face-centered cubic EuN, provides compelling evidence for the formation of highly crystalline EuN nanoparticles dispersed within a carbon matrix. The apparent structural difference between EuNCD and the other samples (NCD and TbNCD) underscores the profound impact of rare earth metal doping on the morphology and crystallinity of carbon dots.

The dual-doped carbon dots (NCD) synthesized in this study exhibit unique photoluminescence properties due to the incorporation of nitrogen and rare earth metals (Terbium and Europium), which make them highly promising for biosensing applications. With tunable emission characteristics, these engineered NCD offer significant potential for developing sensitive and selective biosensors. A specific application of these NCD is illustrated in detecting alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), a biomarker associated with various cancers, particularly hepatocellular carcinoma. The luminescence spectra in Fig. 7a show a prominent emission peak centered at approximately 445 nm, characteristic of carbon dots, likely due to the radiative recombination of excitons at the surface states. As AFP concentration increases from 0 to 3.0 ng/mL, there is a corresponding rise in luminescence intensity at 445 nm, indicating a direct interaction between the NCD and AFP molecules. This intensity enhancement can be attributed to several mechanisms: surface passivation where AFP molecules bind to the NCD surface, reducing non-radiative recombination pathways and thus enhancing fluorescence; Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) where interaction facilitates energy transfer from the NCD to specific fluorophores in the AFP structure, enhancing overall fluorescence; and Aggregation-Induced Emission (AIE) where AFP presence induces slight NCD aggregation, restricting intramolecular rotations and vibrations, thereby increasing fluorescence. The calibration curve in Fig. 7b shows a linear relationship between luminescence intensity and AFP concentration in the 0.01 to 3.0 ng/mL range, suggesting the sensor's high sensitivity and quantitative accuracy.

The estimated detection limit of 0.01 ng/mL is crucial for early-stage disease diagnosis, highlighting the NCD' potential for cancer diagnostics. Furthermore, the specificity of response to AFP implies good selectivity, though further studies are necessary to confirm this against other biomolecules. The potential applications of these NCD extend beyond cancer diagnostics; they are suitable for point-of-care testing, offering rapid, on-site results in clinical settings, and continuous monitoring, allowing real-time tracking of AFP levels, which is valuable for assessing treatment efficacy and disease progression. Additionally, the tunable emission properties of differently doped NCD can facilitate multiplex biosensing, enabling the simultaneous detection of multiple biomarkers. This versatility is further enhanced by rare earth metal doping, opening avenues for theranostics, where diagnostic capabilities are combined with therapeutic applications such as photodynamic therapy. The potential for environmental monitoring is also notable, as the same principles can be applied to detect pollutants or pathogens in water or food samples, ensuring safety and compliance with health standards.

In conclusion, the dual-doped NCD exhibit high sensitivity, a wide linear range, and promising selectivity, making them excellent candidates for next-generation diagnostic tools. However, further studies on their selectivity, stability, and performance in complex biological matrices are essential to realize their clinical potential fully. The promising results demonstrated in this study pave the way for future research and development, aiming to harness the unique properties of these NCD for advanced biosensing applications, potentially transforming the landscape of medical diagnostics and environmental monitoring. Integrating these NCD into practical biosensing devices could significantly enhance early disease detection, monitoring, and treatment, ultimately improving patient outcomes and public health. Therefore, ongoing and future research should focus on optimizing the synthesis process, enhancing the specificity and stability of the NCD, and validating their performance in real-world biological and environmental samples. This comprehensive approach will ensure the transition of these innovative nanomaterials from the laboratory to clinical and field applications, fulfilling their potential as powerful tools in modern science and technology.

Fig. 8 involves a hydrothermal method to synthesize dual-doped carbon dots. This process utilizes organic precursors to form carbon dots doped with metal ions—terbium (Tb) and europium (Eu). These carbon dots are synthesized by subjecting the precursors to elevated temperature and pressure conditions in an aqueous medium, promoting the incorporation of the metal ions during the carbonization process. This results in the formation of luminescent carbon dots that exhibit distinct optical properties when doped with Eu and Tb, as evidenced by the bright emissions observed under UV light. The hydrothermal method allows for controlling particle size and surface chemistry, providing favorable conditions for the dual doping of the carbon dots. The schematic band diagram represents the transition states of energy levels and possible electronic transitions occurring within the synthesized dual-doped carbon dots under illumination. When the material is exposed to incident photon energy, represented by (hυ), electrons are excited from the ground state to a higher energy state (π-π*). This initial excitation sets the stage for various relaxation and emission pathways that ultimately give rise to the luminescent properties of the material. The energy levels illustrated in the band diagram include the excited state (π-π*) and other states linked to the presence of dopants and functional groups. The diagram also shows interstate transitions, where energy is transferred between different states, leading to emission. Specifically, a level attributed to non-bonding orbitals (n) is indicated, related to amino groups present in the material. These amino groups introduce additional states that influence the luminescent properties by offering alternative pathways for electron relaxation. The presence of the dopant ions, Tb and Eu, adds further complexity to the emission process. The involvement of these lanthanide ions in the carbon dot structure creates energy levels associated with the metal-nitrogen bonds (Tb-N and Eu-N), which play a crucial role in the observed luminescence. The emission pathways illustrated in the diagram include direct band-to-band transitions and relaxation through intermediate states. The green and blue arrows signify emissions at different wavelengths, which correspond to the characteristic luminescence of the terbium and europium ions. These distinct emissions result from the various energy transitions facilitated by each dopant. Tb generally emits in the green region, while Eu emits in the red or blue region, depending on the energy transition involved. The materials labeled as NCDN/Eu-N and NCDN/Tb-N demonstrate luminescent behavior under UV light, as shown in the lower part of the image. These luminescent images suggest efficient energy transfer between the carbon dot matrix and the dopant ions, resulting in visible emission. The dual doping enhances the emission properties of the carbon dots and introduces tunability, allowing different colors of luminescence based on the dopants used. In summary, the band diagram represents the energy states and electronic transitions that contribute to the luminescence of the dual-doped carbon dots. Terbium and europium as dopants are crucial in facilitating energy relaxation and subsequent emission, resulting in the fluorescence observed when the material is illuminated with UV light. Combining carbon dots with lanthanide ions presents a promising approach for developing materials with unique luminescent properties suitable for various applications, such as sensing and bioimaging.

Fig. 9 shows the impact of NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD on S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans growth. In the control sample, S. aureus overgrew, reaching its highest optical density at λ=600 nm (OD600) value at 3.435 nm. Changes were seen upon adding NCD, and an OD600 measurement of 1.203 nm was made. The difference between the synthesized TbNCD and EuNCD's OD600 values was 0.855 and 0.895 nm, respectively, which revealed the poorest results, indicating the influence of repression on S. aureus growth as shown in Fig. 9 (a). The same condition was observed in the growth of E. coli, where the growth of non-treated E. coli reached its highest optical density at λ=600 nm (OD600) value at 3.843 nm, and growth inhibition was seen upon the addition of NCD. An OD600 measurement of 1.654 nm was observed. The difference between the synthesized TbNCD and EuNCD' OD600 values was 0.983 and 0.745 nm, respectively, which revealed the promising inhibition and indicated the influence of repression on E. coli growth, as shown in Fig. 9(b). Finally, the growth of non-treated C. albicans reached its highest optical density at λ=600 nm (OD600) value at 3.256 nm, and the growth inhibition was seen upon the addition of NCD. An OD600 measurement of 1.322 nm was observed. The difference between the synthesized TbNCD and EuNCD's OD600 values was 0.845 and 0.802 nm, respectively, which revealed the promising inhibition and indicated the influence of repression on C. albicans growth as shown in Fig. 9(c).

In comparison to NCD, which special synergistic effects may define, the effect of doped Tb, and Eu, and the impact of small particle size as detailed in our earlier work, TbNCD, and EuNCD more suppressing power [65].

The production of specific ROS on the outer layer of the synthesized Carbon Dots has frequently been reported in earlier research [66,67]. ROS produced by the synthesized Carbon Dots can kill bacteria by causing lipid peroxidation, DNA damage, and protein oxidation [68]. Furthermore, although the S. aureus and E coli membranes retain a negative charge, the metal ions produced by the synthesized Carbon Dots (Tb2+ and Eu2+) have a positive charge. This indicates they arise in response to halting bacterial DNA replication, protein denaturation, and bacterial cell disintegration [69]. The increased sensitivity of Gram-positive bacteria to the produced Carbon Dots may be due to their membranes' increased rigidity [70].

Other factors that could make it easier for Carbon Dots to attach themselves to Gram-positive bacteria include their size, shape, and surface charge. According to Xu et al., some produced NPs broke the E. coli membrane after 80 min of UV irradiation [71]. Suggesting that disinfection had finished. Several investigations revealed that most Carbon Dots have antibacterial ability against various bacterial strains, such as S. aureus and E. coli [57,72–74].

The ability of UV light to deactivate S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans is shown in Figs. 10 (a, b and c), respectively, and the degree of sensitivity rises with exposure duration. Throughout the display duration, there were favorable effects on the adhesion and proliferation of S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans (0 to 90 min with 15 min time increments). After being treated with TbNCD and EuNCD, the growth of S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans was significantly suppressed compared to the untreated microbes and NCD. Due to the deactivation following UV light, the bacterial growth in the UV experiment ended at the lowest level. It was determined that exposure to UV light would enhance the possibility of photo-activation of TbNCD and EuNCD; also, the likelihood of TbNCD and EuNCD was greater than that of NCD. Once activated by UV radiation, TbNCD and EuNCD function as highly effective disinfectants. Tb, and Eu dopped on NCD absorb photons and have a role in the production of new ROS (O2 − and H2O2) as well as active hydroxyl (.OH) when O2 and H2O are present (in the atmosphere and/or water states). One way to see microbial decontamination is as ROS (H2O2) interacting with the membranes. After the microbes penetrated, the effective barrier was seen, and the oxidative hydroxyl free radicals remained stable and vigorous.

The protein outflow amounts in the treated solutions of S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans were determined using the Bradford method. According to Fig. 11(a) , the quantity of NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD (in different concentrations) directly correlates with the amount of S. aureus protein removed. The counts were 80.44, 120.34, and 129.54 µg/mL following incubation with NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD, respectively (1.0 mg/mL).

Moreover, Fig. 11(b) illustrates how the concentration of NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD is directly correlated with the amount of E. coli protein removed, with counts of 90.45, 127.55, and 130.43 µg/mL following implementation with NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD, respectively (at 1.0 mg/mL). Finally, Fig. 11(c) showed that, after the C. albicans treatment with NCD, TbNCD, and EuNCD, the protein concentrations were counted to be 93.43, 133.03, and 133.43 µg/mL, respectively.

This depicts the formation of holes in the membranes of S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans, which help to cause the proteins to leak out of the bacterial and yeast cytoplasm and exhibit the antibacterial and antifungal characteristics of TbNCD, and EuNCD more than NCD.

The results showed that TbNCD and EuNCD improved the dissolution of S. aureus, E. coli, and C. albicans membranes, changing their ability to leak over NCD alone. The main factor inhibiting bacterial and yeast growth is the metal's beneficial effect on bacterial and yeast membrane permeability, ultimately leading to protein leakage.

Similar outcomes were observed following nanoparticle treatment, indicating a concentration-dependent effect on bacterial membrane disruption. This process also revealed the leakage of bacterial internal organelles through the compromised external cell structure

This study investigates the synthesis and characterization of dual-doped carbon dots (CD) via the hydrothermal method using various precursor combinations, focusing on their photoluminescence and antimicrobial properties. By systematically varying dopant ratios, the study demonstrates how compositions can engineer CD with tailored optical and functional properties, enabling diverse applications. Malic acid-oxalic acid co-doped exhibited a bathochromic shift in emission from 443 nm to 477 nm as oxalic acid content increased, attributed to surface defects and carboxyl group-induced emissive traps. Similarly, increasing malic acid content caused a red-shift due to hydroxyl and carbonyl contributions to surface states. In contrast, diammonium tartrate-oxalic acid co-doped NCD showed a slight hypsochromic shift from 478 nm to 475 nm with higher oxalic acid, likely due to sp³ defects restricting π-conjugation. Notably, the nitrogen-rich diammonium tartrate system consistently yielded higher emission intensities (30,620–66,260 counts) compared to malic acid-oxalic acid (6260–42,708 counts). Rare earth metal doping (terbium and europium) introduced crystalline terbium nitride (TbN) and europium nitride (EuN) phases, confirmed by XRD. These dopants expanded optical properties by creating new radiative pathways. FT-IR and electron microscopy revealed the dopants’ impact on surface chemistry, morphology, and crystallinity, with particle sizes ranging from 3 to 9 nm. The NCD demonstrated significant antimicrobial activity under UV illumination, attributed to nitrogen and rare-earth doping, enhancing reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation. These findings highlight dual-doped NCD as promising materials for bioimaging, sensing, disinfection, and optoelectronic applications. Future research should focus on scalability, broader dopant combinations, and real-world validations. This study provides a framework for rationally designing advanced nanomaterials with enhanced photoluminescent and antimicrobial capabilities, paving the way for next-generation multifunctional materials.
